In the pre-Unraveling evacuation fragments, it had been Route Nine approach corridor, civic shelter access, late-cascade phase.

Every name carried a claim.

That was the first problem.

The second was that everyone who knew how to reach it had excellent reasons to distrust everyone else.

The meeting took place in the relay market before opening hours. Vendors watched from shuttered stalls. The floor was still wet from cleaning, though Lian suspected the cleaning had been done mostly to show that the market could prepare a room without conceding anyone else owned the terms. Mara and Tovin sat near the back with the account stone in a padded case between them. Fay stood at the midpoint, where she could see every exit. Iris brought the registry annex. Corin brought no map until asked. Pella attended through a remote field unit because the witness circle still needed medical command. Venn came in person with no escort and caused more alarm that way.

The Unwoven broker guild sent three representatives.

The first was Orin Sect's niece, Veyra Sect, who had inherited the family gift for looking amused while calculating exits. The second was a relay engineer named Hobb, old enough to have repaired corridors under three legal regimes and bitter enough to have opinions about all of them. The third was a quiet woman introduced only as Pell, no relation to the other Pells and proof that the universe had begun mocking the record.

Veyra opened with money.

"Fragments are not free."

Iris opened with standing.

"Warnings are not private goods when they indicate active harm."

Veyra smiled. "I can tell this will be friendly."

Fay said, "Terms first. Insults after."

That became the meeting's tone.

The guild would not surrender fragments to Archive custody. The field team would not allow fragments to disappear into private sale. The market holders wanted compensation, defense against retroactive criminalization, and the right to keep local copies. The registry wanted shared custody, provenance chains, and consent review for any living holders. Venn wanted enough access to block Marr's renewed seal attempt. Pella wanted medical review before anyone approached an active shelter trace. Lian wanted three days with the fragments and no one using the phrase "quick scan" within earshot.

Corin wanted to reach Route Nine without turning the approach into a raid.

No one cared what Corin wanted until the bargaining stalled.

Then Veyra looked at him. "You have been quiet for someone whose face makes committees nervous."

"I am trying to improve."

"Shame. Famous people are useful when predictable."

"I disappoint strategically."

Veyra's smile became almost real. "What are you offering?"

"Not trust."

That quieted the table.

"Good," Veyra said. "Trust is what people request when they lack collateral."

Corin placed his hand-drawn map on the table, face down.

Iris looked at him sharply.

He did not turn it over. "This is the map I received from the deep Layer after Sable's indexing fold opened the first descent. It is incomplete, interpretive, and dangerous if treated as command. I will not give it to you. I will let you compare your approach fragments against it here, under shared record, with every mismatch logged. You keep your fragments. We keep the comparison. No one leaves with sole custody of the route."

Veyra studied him. "And what do you get?"

"A path to the second site that does not begin by stealing from the people who preserved it badly but preserved it."

Hobb grunted. "Badly?"

Lian answered. "You sold an extinction warning as charms."

"And your Archive sealed evacuation records until soldiers thought they were weapons."

"Yes."

He blinked, thrown off by agreement.

Lian continued. "We are all doing badly. The distinction is whether we stop making that the end of the analysis."

The quiet woman named Pell spoke for the first time. "If we take you to the Underthread, Archive security follows."

Venn answered. "Not under my authority."

Veyra laughed. "Your authority is not the only authority."

"No," Venn said. "That is why I came without escort."

"Symbolic."

"Vulnerable."

Veyra looked at her for a long moment. "Better."

The terms took five hours.

They drafted a coalition before anyone was willing to use the word. Market holders, registry, Healer Corps, eastern observers, Unwoven relay engineers, Council oversight, and one Null witness team who joined after hearing there would be a corridor everyone agreed was dangerous and therefore insisted someone suspicious should come. No military Reckoner units inside the Underthread. No fragment seizure. No raw route publication. Local copies preserved. Any active Route Nine response logged in shared custody. Any living holder could withdraw their artifact from direct contact unless withdrawal would immediately endanger others, in which case the reason had to be recorded and reviewed.

"This is ugly," Veyra said, reading the final terms.

Iris took the slate. "Yes."

"Expensive."

Fay said, "Also yes."

"Slow."

Lian said, "Tragically not slow enough."

Mara read the section about Tovin's account stone twice. "He can come to the outer approach only."

Pella's remote unit brightened. "Medical agrees."

Tovin objected. Everyone ignored him until Iris asked whether his objection should be recorded.

"Yes," he said. "Record that everyone is being unfair because I found the stone."

Iris recorded it exactly.

The account stone warmed once inside its case.

No one interpreted that as agreement. Everyone noticed.

At 1600, Corin turned over his map.

The room leaned in despite itself.

Veyra placed three Unwoven fragments beside the western knot. Hobb added a relay maintenance strip. Pell added a narrow piece of black glass she had kept hidden until that moment. Its surface carried the strongest white-blue trace Lian had seen outside the formation.

"Where did you get that?" Lian asked.

Pell looked at the glass, not at her. "My mother used it to find people who did not want to be found."

Fay went still.

"Bounty routing?" Veyra asked softly.

Pell nodded.

The room absorbed another way the warning had been used.

Safe return. Account correction. Trade charm. Bounty routing. Every fragment had survived by becoming useful, and every use had bent the warning further from its origin.

When the black glass touched Corin's map, the western knot completed.

Not as a location alone. As a set of terms.

The second site would open only through a corridor of repeated returns. Anyone entering with a single objective would be routed back to the last point where they had refused cost. Anyone entering with extraction authority might never notice they were repeating. Anyone entering without local fragment holders would not reach the shelter door at all.

Lian read the geometry three times before speaking.

"The corridor tests what you are trying to avoid."

Veyra's amusement vanished. "That sounds like a story told to make a trap feel wise."

"It may be a trap."

"But wise?"

"No. Old."

Corin thought of the costless door's message: when cost returned, they revised again. Route Nine's approach corridor might still carry that behavior. Not consciousness, perhaps. Not intent. Infrastructure trained by a vanished civilization to route people away from what they could not bear to pay.

That made it more dangerous than a locked door.

A locked door said no.

This one might say yes forever.

The coalition terms were signed at dusk.

No one called the group allies. Veyra said ally was a word people used before betrayal to make betrayal sound dramatic. Iris proposed co-belligerents against noncarrying. Lian threatened to leave. Fay wrote "Route Nine provisional party" on the working file and dared anyone to improve it. No one did.

As they prepared to leave, Marr's new motion reached Venn's slate.

She read it and went pale with anger.

"He is proposing conditional seal if any Unwoven-held fragment proves active."

Veyra looked unsurprised. "Then he will have to seal half the market."

"He might try."

"Then bring better law."

Corin folded his map. The western knot remained visible in his mind now, completed by market fragments and stained by every use the warning had survived.

The second deep-layer site waited under contested territory.

To reach it, they would need the Archive's records, the Unwoven's fragments, the eastern communities' standing, the Null's suspicion, the Healer Corps' refusal protocols, the registry's custody, and people like Veyra who trusted no one enough to make the coalition honest.

Fay walked beside Corin toward the market exit.

"This is going to fail at least twice," she said.

"Only twice?"

"I was being kind."

Outside, the relay market opened for evening trade. People moved around them carrying food, contraband, memory, lies, and pieces of a warning older than every border in the room. No one stopped selling because the world had become morally complex. The world had always been morally complex. The Route Nine reckoning merely removed another excuse for pretending otherwise.

At the western edge of Harrow, beneath roads no current map admitted were roads, Route Nine waited with its costless door.

This time, they would not let anyone enter it alone.

The first failure came before they left the market.

Veyra tried to substitute a newer refusal token for the old one Fay had requested. It was a beautiful piece: black metal, clean stamp, authenticated by three guild marks and expensive enough to make a Council auditor faint. Lian scanned it, frowned, and said it was dead.

Veyra looked offended. "It is valid."

"Commercially, probably. Structurally, no. It has no use-wear."

"Use-wear lowers value."

"Then value killed it."

Hobb laughed so loudly two vendors opened their shutters to check whether a fight had become entertaining.

Fay asked to see the old tokens.

Veyra said no.

Mara said, "Then enjoy your valid object and your useless expedition."

The room turned toward her.

She lifted both hands. "What? I sell accounts, not patience."

The old tokens arrived an hour later in a wooden box carried by Pell, the quiet broker with the bounty-routing glass. They were ugly. Scratched bone, cracked ceramic, one strip of stamped leather so worn the refusal mark had almost vanished. Each had been used to break a contract without triggering blood debt. Each carried a story of someone deciding survival was not worth the price being asked.

Fay selected none of them.

"The expedition cannot choose its own refusal anchor," she said. "That defeats the purpose."

Veyra's eyes narrowed. "Then who chooses?"

The market holders argued for twenty minutes. Not the brokers. The holders: vendors, couriers, debt witnesses, repair people, Mara, the nameless soup owner, two elders who had appeared from nowhere as soon as the word old tokens entered the room. They chose the leather strip.

Its story was simple. A courier had used it thirty-four years earlier to refuse a return route that would have saved her cargo by abandoning three injured workers outside the corridor's protection. She lost the contract, kept the workers alive, and spent twelve years paying the debt.

"Where is she?" Iris asked.

"Dead," said the soup owner. "Peacefully, which annoyed her enemies."

The leather strip became the refusal anchor.

The return object remained Tovin's account stone, held by Mara under shared custody.

The cost witness would be Mara herself, with Tovin's objection attached and Pella's medical veto recorded.

The expedition party shifted again around those terms. Corin, Iris, Lian, Fay, Mara, Veyra, Hobb, Pell, one Null witness, Dena for affected-community standing, and Cross for procedural record. Pella would remain at the field command relay unless medical conditions forced her forward. Venn would hold the Council side. Renn Vale would keep military units outside the Underthread unless active harm crossed the perimeter.

No one liked the party.

That was why it might work.

They reached the Underthread entrance at dawn.

It lay beneath a collapsed freight arch west of Harrow, hidden behind market refuse, old warning paint, and a century of people agreeing not to ask why the air there felt colder. Hobb opened the first gate with a tool that looked illegal in every possible jurisdiction. Fay held the witness lamp. Iris opened the route log. Mara carried the account stone. The leather refusal strip hung from Dena's wrist because the market holders had decided an affected-community witness should carry the right to say no.

Veyra objected to that until Mara asked whether she preferred the Null witness.

Veyra withdrew the objection.

The corridor beyond the gate sloped into darkness.

The Route Nine mark appeared on the left wall after twelve steps.

Not scratched. Built into the structure. White-blue residue moved under its surface like a memory of water.

Lian scanned it and whispered, "Active."

The account stone warmed.

The leather strip tightened around Dena's wrist.

No one moved.

Corin felt the corridor notice them.

Not as a mind. As infrastructure trained by vanished need. A road built to return people safely and then used until safe return became a question no one could answer without losing part of what made answer meaningful.

The first test came as a sound from behind them: market noise, bright and close, as if the entrance were only one step away.

Mara turned.

Fay caught her elbow without gripping. "Choice."

Mara closed her eyes. "I know."

The sound became Tovin's voice. "Grandmother?"

Pella's remote unit crackled with medical alarm. "Mara, state orientation."

Mara's face twisted. "I am in the Underthread. Tovin is not here. The corridor is offering return through fear."

The leather strip warmed.

Dena lifted her wrist. "Refusal anchor active."

Mara did not turn back. She stood until Tovin's false voice faded.

The corridor let them proceed.

Veyra looked less amused now.

"That was cheap," she said.

Hobb answered, "Old systems do not need new tricks if the old ones work."

They walked deeper.

At the second mark, the corridor offered Corin the eastern field before extraction. A chance to accept the military clamp, preserve Sable's individual signal, prevent shock injuries, keep the bridge closed until proper authority arrived. It came not as temptation but as administrative reasonableness. A better sequence. Fewer immediate harms. A future without mud, fractures, and Lian's face when Sable vanished.

He stopped.

Iris saw. "What cost is it hiding?"

The question cut through the offered return.

Corin looked at the false sequence and found the absence: uncarried records never surfacing, Route Nine never discovered, the warning sealed in a corridor that harmed no one visibly because it reached nothing dangerous enough to change them.

"It hides the people we would not have carried," he said.

The corridor dimmed.

The leather strip warmed again.

They proceeded.

By the third mark, no one thought of the Underthread as merely a road.

It was a machine for offering the version of return each traveler most wanted to call responsible.

That made every step a negotiation with self-flattery.

At the old service junction, the corridor opened into a chamber whose ceiling vanished into dark. The western knot glowed on the far wall. Beneath it, three route lines converged: Archive freight substrate, Unwoven relay path, pre-Unraveling shelter access.

The second deep-layer site lay below.

The account stone pulsed once.

The refusal strip went still.

The corridor had brought them to the door.

Now they had to decide whether safe return was still one of the terms they were willing to accept.
